text: Figure AI just achieved something robotics engineers have been chasing for years. True 24/7 autonomous operation. Figure03 is now working around the clock with zero human supervision. Not just during normal business hours, but continuously through the night, early mornings, weekends, and even holidays. Whether it's 2 a.m. on a Sunday or Christmas Day, the system keeps running without pause. Hey guys, Alfie here. Welcome back to AI Nexus. So, here's what just dropped. Figure posted a short clip about 20 seconds showing multiple Figure 03 robots moving through their facility. These tall black and white humanoid machines are walking smoothly across a wide open floor, navigating the space, avoiding each other, and not a single person is stepping in to help. Some robots are moving, others are parked in the background, probably charging or on standby. It's a quiet, almost eerie look at what a robotrun workspace actually looks like, but the video is just the surface. The real story is what makes this non-stop operation possible. Running robots 24/7, nights, weekends, holidays without anyone babysitting them. That was described as really, really hard to pull off. Think about what that actually means. These machines need to manage their own energy, handle their own problems, and coordinate with each other, all without a human stepping in. And the biggest challenge in all of that, battery life. Here's how they solved it. When a robot's battery starts running low, it sends a message to another robot that's already sitting at the charging dock. That docked robot heads out and picks up the work right where the first one left off. Meanwhile, the low battery robot walks itself to the dock and starts charging. No downtime, no gaps. One steps out, the other steps in, like a relay race that never ends. And it gets smarter than that. If a robot runs into any kind of issue, whether it's a software glitch or a hardware hiccup, it doesn't just freeze in the middle of the floor. It walks itself over to a dedicated triage area, kind of like a pit stop. Engineers can check what went wrong, fix it, and improve the system for next time. And while that robot is getting looked at, another one swaps in immediately to keep the work going. The whole operation just keeps moving forward no matter what. Even the charging tech is worth mentioning. These robots use wireless inductive charging built right into their feet. They step onto a special pad on the floor, and coils in the feet connect to coils in the pad. It charges at up to 2 kW and a full charge takes about an hour. No plugs, no [music] cables, just step on and power up. It's the kind of small detail that makes true autonomy actually work in practice. And this isn't just a lab demo anymore. This system is already running across multiple real use cases, including customer deployments. We're talking warehouse work, factory floors, and even

cables, just step on and power up. It's the kind of small detail that makes true autonomy actually work in practice. And this isn't just a lab demo anymore. This system is already running across multiple real use cases, including customer deployments. We're talking warehouse work, factory floors, and even robots greet people and have actual conversations with them. That's a big shift from cool prototype in a lab to this thing is out in the world doing real work. The autonomy angle here is what really stands out. Figure isn't just building robots. They're building a system where robots manage themselves. They charge themselves. They replace each other. They report their own problems. That's a whole different level. And it points to something bigger. A future where entire operation, factories, warehouses, maybe even retail spaces run continuously without human oversight. Now, none of this would be possible without the brain running inside these robots. And that brain is called Helix 02. If you caught our earlier breakdown, you already know Helix 02 is a massive leap [music] forward. But for those just joining, here's why it matters so much, especially for what we just saw. For decades, robotics worked by splitting everything into separate systems. One controller for walking, another for the arms, another for [music] balance. It's like playing a video game where three different people each control one button. And if they're not perfectly in sync, the whole thing falls apart. Figure through that entire approach out. They replaced over a 100,000 lines of handwritten code with a single neural network. One unified system that controls everything. The legs, the torso, the head, the arms, and all 10 fingers. That's Helix 02. And this is exactly why these robots can run autonomously around the clock. Helix 02 isn't just handling one task at a time. It's managing long chains of actions. walking to a location, picking something up, adjusting balance, recovering from small mistakes, all without freezing or needing a reset. In robotics, they call this long horizon control. A short task is something like, "Pick up that cup. Easy. Only a few steps, high chance of success. A long task is completely different. It's dozens of decisions chained together over minutes of continuous movement. Walking, reaching, adjusting grip, shifting weight, all flowing into each other without stopping. And that's where most robots completely fall apart. Even one tiny error early on can snowball and collapse the whole sequence. But Helix 02 keeps track of everything happening across the body, adjusts in real time, and pushes forward. It doesn't freeze, doesn't [music] reset, it just keeps going. That's the difference between a robot that can do a trick and one that can actually finish a job. The system runs on three layers, and each one handles a different level of thinking. The first layer is like muscle memory. It handles balance and posture a thousand times per second. You don't think about how to

can do a trick and one that can actually finish a job. The system runs on three layers, and each one handles a different level of thinking. The first layer is like muscle memory. It handles balance and posture a thousand times per second. You don't think about how to it. That's what this layer does for the robot. It's the reason the robot doesn't tip over when it's carrying something heavy or nudging a door shut with its hip. The second layer connects the cameras and touch sensors directly to the joints, so the robot can see something and react to it physically at the same time. It runs at 200 times per second, fast enough to catch a wobble or adjust a grip before anything goes wrong. And the top layer understands language. You can tell it walk to the charging dock or go greet the visitor at the front desk. And it breaks that big goal down into smaller steps for the rest of the system to execute. It doesn't need to know the exact angle of every joint. It just understands what you want and figures out how to make it happen. This layered setup is what allows these robots to make smart decisions on their own, like knowing when to head back to charge or when to call for a replacement. It's not pre-programmed logic. It's learned behavior. And that's a massive difference. Now, there's one more piece of this puzzle that often gets overlooked, and it might be the most important one. The hands. Figure just revealed their seventh generation robotic hand. And here's a number that puts things in perspective. They've built three different robot bodies during the same time, but seven versions of the hand. That tells you exactly where the hardest engineering problem is. The human hand has 27 degrees of freedom. That means 27 independent ways your fingers and thumb can move. Most robots in the industry skip that complexity entirely and just use basic grippers. Clamp down, pick up, done. Figure is going the hard route. They're building hands that actually work like ours. And if you look at the progress across generations, it's [music] been fast. The first generation had simple gripping tools, functional but limited. The second moved up to 16° of freedom per hand with the strength to lift about 55 lbs. The Figure03 hand added palm mounted cameras and touch sensors sensitive enough to detect a force of just three grams. That's lighter than a sugar packet sitting on your fingertip. This new 7th generation hand pushes even further. We're looking at over 20° of freedom, tighter joint movements, stronger motors, and more advanced touch feedback. The goal is straightforward, but incredibly hard to achieve. Pick up a fragile egg without cracking it, fold a piece of clothing, and then turn around and grip a heavy power tool, all with the same hand. And this hand hardware is a huge part of why Helix 02

feedback. The goal is straightforward, but incredibly hard to achieve. Pick up a fragile egg without cracking it, fold a piece of clothing, and then turn around and grip a heavy power tool, all with the same hand. And this hand hardware is a huge part of why Helix 02 palm cameras mean the robot can see what its fingers are doing even when its own arm blocks the view from the head cameras. The fingertip sensors let it feel how hard it's gripping something in real time. Feed all of that sensor data into Helix 02's neural network and you get a robot that doesn't just move through space. It feels its way through a task, adjusting grip and pressure on the fly. Other companies are chasing the same goal. Sanctuary AI already has hands with 21 degrees of freedom. But what sets Figure apart is the combination. The hand hardware paired with an AI brain like Helix that can actually learn new tasks without needing to be reprogrammed every single time. That's what turns a capable hand into a useful one. When you put all of this together, the autonomous fleet management, the Helix 02 brain, and these next level hands, you start to see the full picture. This isn't just about one cool demo or one flashy video anymore. This is a complete system designed to work in the real world, doing real jobs without [music] ever stopping. Factories that run through the night, warehouses that never shut down, customer sites that stay operational on weekends and holidays, and eventually maybe even your home. Figure is building toward a future where machines don't clock out. And based on everything we just saw, that future is a lot closer than most people realize. Engine AI is taking T800 robots into a completely new arena. While Boston Dynamics showed the world what controlled agility looks like, and Unit's G1 proved how fast lowcost humanoids are catching up. Engine AI has officially unleashed the T800 robot, and they've built a literal humanoid robot combat league for it. On February 9th, Engine AI officially announced the world's first commercial humanoid free combat league. Think of it like the UFC, [music] but instead of flesh and bone, it's 165 lbs of aviation grade aluminum and high torque actuators colliding inside the ring. The scale of this is insane. We're talking about a tiered competition running through December of this year where 16 teams ranging from elite university labs to hardcore robotic startups [music] are going head-to-head. And here is the kicker. Engine AI is providing the T800 robots to these teams free of charge. They want the smartest minds on the planet to take their hardware and program the most lethal robot kung fu algorithms possible. Why would a company give away millions of dollars in hardware just to watch it get beat up in an octagon? Because of the 1.44 million prize. The winning team gets a 10 kg

the planet to take their hardware and program the most lethal robot kung fu algorithms possible. But the real goal isn't the gold. It's the data. Engine AI knows that if a robot can survive a roundhouse kick to the chassis and stay standing, it can survive anything a factory floor or a disaster zone can throw at it. These matches aren't just for show. They follow a best of three format. If a robot gets knocked down and can't stand up within 10 seconds, that's a knockout. If the human operators have to step in and reset the bot twice, that's a technical knockout. We are seeing real-time strategy where humans guide the highle tactics. But the T800's onboard AI handles the balance, [music] the striking, and the survival instincts. Experts are terrified because this isn't just entertainment. It's a high-speed evolution of predatory movement in machines. Now, if you're thinking, Alfie, this has to be CGI, right? You aren't alone. Back when the first T800 promos dropped in late 2025, the internet exploded with claims that the footage was fake. The movements were too fluid, the spinning kicks too perfect. So, [music] Engine AI CEO Xiao Tongyang did something legendary, or maybe just crazy. He didn't release a statement. He stepped into the ring. In a video that is now dominating every tech platform, Xiao suited up in heavy protective gear and stood directly in front of his creation. The footage is haunting. You see the T800 stabilize its stance, calculate the distance, and fire off a high torque sidekick. The impact didn't just move him. It lifted the CEO off the ground and dropped him straight onto the mat. No V effects, no wires, just pure mechanical force. After the strike, the T800 didn't wobble or glitch. Its gyroscopic sensors and real-time control loops reset its balance in milliseconds. [music] Xiao's reaction after the test, too violent, too brutal. Without this gear, anyone would break a bone. He joked that he couldn't guarantee he'd stay alive for the next test. It was a terrifyingly effective way to shut down the doubters. This wasn't a publicity stunt. It was a stress demonstration. It proved that the T800 is built for unpredictability and massive impact, making it the perfect athlete for the URL Combat League. So, what makes the T800 capable of this level of violence? Let's look at the engineering because this is where the Terminator inspiration becomes reality. First, the name isn't just a marketing gimmick. Engine AI borrowed the philosophy of the movie T800, a machine with a strong structure and proportions that mimic an adult human perfectly. This thing is a beast. Torque and power. inspiration becomes reality. First, the name isn't just a marketing gimmick. Engine AI borrowed the philosophy of the movie T800, a machine with a strong structure and proportions that mimic an adult human perfectly. This thing is a beast. Torque and power. Each joint motor delivers up to 450 new that's enough force to generate bone shattering striking momentum. We're talking about an instantaneous peak power output of 14,000 watts. Active cooling. Pushing that much force creates a massive amount of heat. Most robots would melt their own circuits or have to take a break. The T800 features a worldirst active cooling system built directly into its leg joints. This prevents thermal throttling, allowing the robot to fight or work for up to 4 hours without stopping. Energy system. It's powered by a solidstate lithium battery architecture. This is safer and more energy dense than standard batteries. Designed specifically for highintensity sessions where a performance drop isn't an option. The brain, it's running on the Nvidia Jetson Thor chip, delivering a staggering 2,000 tops of AI compute power. This allows for millisecond level environmental processing through its 360° LAR and stereo depth cameras. It's not just moving, it's perceiving the fight. Engine AI even ditched the robot shuffle. Most humanoids walk with bent knees to stay stable. The T800 aims for straight leg walking, making its gate look eerie and natural. Every microcorrection in its hips and ankles is calculated in real time, allowing for those Capo Air inspired spinning transitions that looked too real to be true. The T800 represents a shift in how we build AI. By moving out of the lab and into the combat arena, Engine AI is accelerating the development cycle of these machines by over 30%. They are planting seeds for a future where robots can handle any physical environment, no matter how chaotic. But it leaves us with a chilling question. If we are training robots to be the ultimate fighters today, what does that mean for their roles in our world tomorrow? Is the T800 a breakthrough in engineering? or are we literally building the foundation for the sci-fi nightmares we've watched on screen for decades? What do you think? Would you step into the ring with AT800, even with the best armor on the planet? [music] And do you think this combat first approach will actually help them build better service robots? ring, Unit's G1 is out in the real world. And it just learned how to skateboard. Unit G1 just learned to [music] skateboard in the real world. Droid up unveiled a hyperrealistic humanoid named Moya that Ping's iron collapsed mid [music] demo in front of a live crowd. Hey guys, Alfie here. Welcome back to the AI Nexus. [music] This week, the line between machine and human shifted again. A humanoid robot just learned to skateboard, not as a staged lab trick, but as a real skill executed in the open world, on solid ground, on asphalt, moving freely without human support. The G1 humanoid from Unitri Robotics pulled it off. And what makes it remarkable isn't just balance. It's the intelligence behind every movement. This breakthrough came from a collaboration between China Telecom's Teleai Institute, Shanghai Gaia University, and several leading Chinese research teams. At the center of the project is a new control framework known as Husky, a physicsaware whole body system [music] built specifically for dynamic tasks like skateboarding. Instead of teaching the robot to replay fixed motions, Husky allows the G1 to reason about how its entire body interacts with the skateboard in real time. Every lean, weight shift, and posture adjustment is mathematically tied to how the board responds underneath. When the robot tilts, it understands how sharply the board will turn and how [music] much force is needed to remain stable. The physics aren't approximated. They're embedded directly into the control logic. Before ever stepping onto a real skateboard, the G1 trained entirely inside a physics simulation. Thousands of parallel training environments ran [music] simultaneously, compressing millions of motion trials into roughly 20 hours of compute time. Once the system stabilized, those learned behaviors were transferred directly into the physical robot using a simreal pipeline. The result is striking. The G1 can push off with one leg. Balance while coasting, steer by shifting weight, and smoothly mount or dismount the board across indoor floors, outdoor pavement, and uneven walkways. Even when nudged midride, it recovers and stays [music] upright. This is the first humanoid robot to achieve realworld skateboarding through reinforcement learning. We just watched a robot learn to skate. What happens when it learns everything else? And while Unirit focused on balance and learning, Boston Dynamics focused on [music] power and performance, Boston Dynamics just dropped footage that completely changes the conversation around humanoid robots. Atlas executed a flawless backflip and landed precisely on its toes with the kind of control [music] you'd expect from an Olympic gymnast, not a 110 lb robot. what makes this absolutely mind-blowing. Rewind just one month to early January 2026 and you'll find a very different story. Boston Dynamics The big reveal happened at CES 2026 in Las Vegas, where Boston Dynamics showcased the productionready electric version of Atlas. This electric model reaches 7.5 ft in height and handles loads up to 110 lb with remarkable precision. Engineers built it [music] to withstand brutal factory conditions, operating smoothly in temperatures ranging from -4° F all the way up to 104°. The real game changer announced at CES [music] was the partnership with Google DeepMind. Boston Dynamics integrated Gemini artificial intelligence directly into Atlas, giving the robot genuine problem solving capabilities. Instead of requiring detailed programming for every task, Atlas can now observe its environment and execute complex sequences independently. This cognitive upgrade means Atlas can master entirely new factory tasks in under 24 hours. Hyundai Motors revealed their aggressive manufacturing timeline at the same event. The company is constructing a facility designed to build 30,000 [music] Atlas units annually with deployment into American factories beginning within the next 2 years. These robots will run continuous 24-hour [music] operations, handling everything from heavy component transport to precision assembly work. Atlas even swaps its own battery packs at charging stations [music] without human intervention. The transformation from shaky prototype to back flipping factory worker happened faster than anyone predicted. While Atlas mastered strength [music] and agility, another company focused on realism. Did China just build the most realistic robot on the planet? That question is suddenly very real because a Shanghai based startup called Droid Up has unveiled a humanoid robot named Moya. And the realism is mind-blowing. Moya stands about [music] 5'5 and weighs just 32 kg, which is shockingly light for a full humanoid robot. That low weight matters because it allows smoother movement, better balance, and safer interaction around people. The first thing everyone [music] notices is the skin. Moya is covered in high-end silicone synthetic skin designed to copy [music] human texture and even human warmth. When you touch the robot, it does not feel cold or metallic. It feels warm, and that instantly changes how your brain reacts. But here's the crazy part. The face is not just animated. Moya's head has 25° of freedom. That means micro expressions, small eyebrow shifts, subtle eye movement, tiny lip changes, the same nonverbal cues people use every day without thinking. This is where the uncanny valley hits hard. Under that lifelike exterior is serious engineering. Moya runs on an upgraded walker skeleton evolved from a humanoid robot that placed third in the world's first humanoid half marathon in Beijing. 13 mi. No shortcuts, real endurance. The new Walker platform improves cooling and long-term movement using lightweight lattice muscle materials, making the robot move naturally for longer periods.

Droid up is aiming directly at elder care, companionship, and everyday human environments. [music] Moya is modular, too. Hair, skin tone, and appearance can be customized without changing the core robot. So, here's the real question. If a robot looks human, [music] feels warm, and reacts emotionally, does that make interaction easier, or deeply uncomfortable? Because robots like Moya are no longer experimental, they are preparing to live around us, which brings us to a very public test in Shenzhen. Xping's iron humanoid robot just faceplanted at a shopping mall in Shenzhen. The robot was walking through the crowd when it suddenly lost balance and fell hard. Cameras caught everything and the video spread fast online, but CEO Hiaoang didn't hide from it. He posted on social media calling it part of the learning process, explaining that realworld testing means dealing with falls and failures. But here's the crazy part. When Iron first appeared in public, people didn't believe it was actually a robot. The movement looked too smooth, too natural. Online comments [music] exploded with accusations that Xping was faking it by putting a human inside a robot suit. So what did Xiaoing do? He literally peeled back the robot skin during a live demonstration and showed everyone the internal structure, motors, wiring, actuators, everything mechanical underneath. So why does Xping make iron look this human? The company wants robots that can use human tools, fit through human doorways, and work in spaces designed for us. To pull that off, iron uses lightweight lattice muscle materials that mimic how real muscles contract and expand, giving it smoother and more efficient movement than older rigid systems. And Xping didn't stop there. The company just launched [music] a quadriped robot designed like a pony. This puts Xping in direct competition with Untitri [music] Dynamics in the four-legged robot space. But Xping's version has something wild, a flexible robotic arm that works like a tail. It can tuck between the legs or extend [music] out to grab objects, open doors, and handle tasks that typical quadripeds can't touch. Xping is pushing hard to make robots that actually work in the real world falls in all. And honestly, that messy, honest approach might be exactly what gets us to a future [music] where robots are everywhere. But while some companies test robots in malls, others introduced them to the entire world. Here's the moment that quietly changed the tone of robotics this week. During the Super Bowl, an event watched by over a 100 million people, Open AI slipped something unexpected into its commercial. Not a humanoid robot on stage, not a sci-fi character, just hands, human hands, and then robotic

Here's the moment that quietly changed the tone of robotics this week. During the Super Bowl, an event watched by over a 100 million people, Open AI slipped something unexpected into its commercial. Not a humanoid robot on stage, not a sci-fi character, just hands, human hands, and then robotic people building, creating, and guiding tools, ending with a pair of robotic arms being directed by human motion. No explosions, no hype characters, just a clear message. You can just build things. But here's the crazy part. That single visual choice signaled something much bigger than an ad for [music] software. Open AI has spent years telling the world it focuses on intelligence, not hardware. Yet placing a bimanual robot in the most valuable advertising slot on Earth was not accidental. The robotic hands were shown as extensions of human intent, not replacements. That framing matters. It positions robots as tools that act through language, instructions, and reasoning, not remote controls or scripts. The ad centered on Codeex, Open AI's coding agent, but the subtext was physical. Software no longer stops at screens. It flows into arms, joints, and real world actions. This lines up with Open AI's recent work on bmanual manipulation where robots respond to natural language and perform coordinated tasks like sorting objects or handling dishes. Even though Open AI no longer partners with figure AI, the direction is clear. And remember where this aired? The Super Bowl is to America what the World Cup or cricket finals are elsewhere. Showing robotic hands here wasn't about developers. It was about normalization, about telling the public that robots are becoming part of everyday creation. The staggering part is what was missing. No uncanny faces, no talking robots, just hands doing work. That restraint suggests confidence. Open AI didn't need to announce a robot. The signal was enough. When robotic hands show up on the biggest stage in the world, the future isn't being teased anymore.

title: Figure AI Just Dropped a FULLY Autonomous AI Robot… It Works 24/7
